notes.section_title: Introduction: Frédéric Sorrieu's Vision and the Concept of Nation

content.text: In 1848, French artist Frédéric Sorrieu created a series of prints envisioning a world composed of 'democratic and social Republics.' His first print shows peoples of Europe and America—men, women, and children of all social classes—marching together in homage to the Statue of Liberty. This vision symbolised fraternity among nations. The broken symbols of absolutist institutions lie in the foreground, representing the overthrow of autocratic rule. Sorrieu's print visualised nations identified by flags and national costumes, with the United States, Switzerland, France, and others leading the procession. This artwork became a powerful representation of how artists of the nineteenth century attempted to give concrete form to abstract ideals of nationalism.

content.title: Key Concept: Personification of Nations
content.text: Nations were portrayed as female figures by artists—abstract ideas given concrete form. This practice made nationalism tangible and emotionally resonant for people.

Nationalism emerged as a powerful force in nineteenth-century Europe that transformed the political and ideological landscape. The concept of a 'nation-state' is central to understanding this period. A nation-state is a form of governance in which a centralised power exercises sovereign control over a clearly defined territory. However, what distinguished the modern nation-state from earlier states was that the majority of citizens, not only rulers, developed a sense of collective identity, shared history, or common descent. This sense of commonness did not exist from time immemorial—it was forged through struggles, actions of leaders, and the participation of common people.

content.title: Ernst Renan's Definition of a Nation (1882)
content.text: French philosopher Ernst Renan argued that a nation is 'the culmination of a long past of endeavours, sacrifice and devotion.' Nations require: (1) common glories in the past, (2) common will in the present, (3) great deeds performed together, and (4) willingness to perform more together. For Renan, 'a nation is a large-scale solidarity' based on daily reaffirmation (plebiscite), and its existence is essential for guaranteeing liberty.

Renan rejected the notion that nationality was determined solely by common language, race, religion, or territory. Instead, he emphasised shared historical experience and collective will. This definition was revolutionary because it placed the people—their choices and commitments—at the centre of nation-building, rather than ethnic or geographic factors alone.

notes.section_title: 1. The French Revolution and the Idea of the Nation

topics.topic_name: How the French Revolution Created National Consciousness

content.text: The first clear expression of nationalism came with the French Revolution in 1789. France was an absolute monarchical state ruled by the king. The revolutionary changes transferred sovereignty from the monarchy to the body of French citizens. The revolutionaries proclaimed that 'the people' would henceforth constitute the nation and shape its destiny.

content.title: Measures Taken to Create Collective Identity
items: La patrie (the fatherland) and le citoyen (the citizen) were emphasised to create a united community of equals enjoying equal rights under a constitution.
A new French flag—the tricolour—replaced the old royal standard as a symbol of national pride.
The Estates General was transformed into the National Assembly, elected by active citizens.
National hymns were composed, patriotic oaths taken, and national martyrs commemorated in the name of the nation.
A centralised administrative system was established with uniform laws for all citizens within French territory.
Internal customs duties and tolls were abolished to facilitate trade and economic unity.
A uniform system of weights and measures was adopted across France.
Regional dialects were discouraged; French as spoken and written in Paris became the common national language.

content.title: Revolutionary Mission
content.text: The French revolutionaries declared it their mission and destiny to liberate the peoples of Europe from despotism and help them become nations. This expansionist ideology of nationalism had profound consequences for European politics.

topics.topic_name: Spread of Revolutionary Ideas: Jacobin Clubs and French Armies

content.text: When news of the French Revolution reached different European cities, students and educated middle-class members began setting up Jacobin clubs. These clubs became centres of nationalist and liberal thought. Their activities prepared the way for French armies that moved into Holland, Belgium, Switzerland, and much of Italy during the 1790s. As revolutionary wars broke out, French armies began carrying the idea of nationalism abroad, spreading revolutionary principles and the concept of popular sovereignty.

The reception of French armies was mixed. Initially, in cities like Brussels, Mainz, Milan, and Warsaw, as well as in regions like Holland and Switzerland, the French were welcomed as harbingers of liberty against monarchy. However, initial enthusiasm soon turned to hostility when it became clear that French rule did not bring political freedom—only increased taxation, censorship, and forced conscription into French armies for further conquests.

notes.section_title: 2. The Making of Nationalism in Europe

content.text: Mid-eighteenth-century Europe had no 'nation-states' as understood today. What we now call Germany, Italy, and Switzerland were divided into kingdoms, duchies, and cantons whose rulers held autonomous territories. Eastern and Central Europe was dominated by autocratic monarchies within which lived diverse peoples who did not see themselves as sharing collective identity or common culture. They spoke different languages and belonged to different ethnic groups. The Habsburg Empire, ruling Austria-Hungary, was a patchwork of many regions and peoples—including Alpine regions (Tyrol, Austria, Sudetenland), Bohemia, Italian-speaking Lombardy and Venetia, Hungary (where Magyars and diverse dialects coexisted), Galicia (Polish aristocracy), and territories of Bohemians, Slovaks, Slovenes, Croats, and Romanians. The only tie binding these diverse groups was common allegiance to the emperor.

content.title: Ethnic and Linguistic Diversity
content.text: The absence of a shared language, culture, or historical narrative made political unity difficult. These diverse populations had no sense of belonging to a single nation. Nationalism would eventually provide an ideology to overcome this fragmentation.

topics.topic_name: 2.1 The Aristocracy and the Emergence of the New Middle Class

content.text: European society in the eighteenth century was dominated by a landed aristocracy united by a common way of life transcending regional divisions. They owned estates in the countryside and town-houses in cities, spoke French for diplomacy and high society, and were often connected by ties of marriage. However, numerically they were a small group. The majority of the population was peasantry. In Western Europe, land was farmed by tenants and small owners; in Eastern and Central Europe, vast estates were cultivated by serfs.

The growth of industrial production and trade in Western and parts of Central Europe led to the emergence of commercial classes whose existence depended on production for the market. Industrialisation began in England in the second half of the eighteenth century but occurred in France and German states only during the nineteenth century. This created new social groups: an industrial working class and a middle class composed of industrialists, businessmen, and professionals. It was among these educated, liberal middle classes that ideas of national unity and the abolition of aristocratic privileges gained popularity.

content.title: Driving Force of Nationalism
content.text: The emerging middle classes became the driving force of nationalist movements. They sought to challenge aristocratic dominance and establish their own political and economic power through the creation of unified nation-states.

Ideas of national unity in early nineteenth-century Europe were closely allied to liberalism. Derived from the Latin 'liber' (free), liberalism for the new middle classes stood for freedom of the individual and equality before the law. Politically, it emphasised government by consent and rejected autocracy, clerical privileges, and absolutism. Nineteenth-century liberals advocated for constitutional government through elected parliament and the inviolability of private property.

However, 'equality before the law' did not necessarily mean universal suffrage. In revolutionary France, which pioneered liberal democracy, the right to vote and hold office was granted exclusively to property-owning men. Men without property and all women were excluded. Only briefly under the Jacobins did all adult males enjoy suffrage. The Napoleonic Code of 1804 reverted to limited suffrage and reduced women to the status of minors, subject to the authority of fathers and husbands.

content.title: Key Aspects of Liberal Nationalism
items: Individual freedom and equality before the law
Government by consent (rejection of autocracy)
Constitutional and representative government through elected parliaments
Inviolability of private property
Freedom of markets and abolition of state-imposed trade restrictions
Limited suffrage (property-owning men only, initially)

content.text: In the German-speaking regions of the early nineteenth century, liberal nationalism expressed itself strongly in economic demands. Napoleon's administrative measures had created a confederation of 39 German states, each with its own currency, weights, and measures. For merchants travelling from Hamburg to Nuremberg in 1833, passage meant crossing 11 customs barriers, paying a customs duty of approximately 5 per cent at each. Weights and measures varied by region: an 'elle' (cloth measure) measured different lengths in different cities—54.7 cm in Frankfurt, 55.1 cm in Mainz, 65.6 cm in Nuremberg, and 53.5 cm in Freiburg. This fragmentation created enormous obstacles to trade and economic growth.

The new commercial classes argued that a unified economic territory allowing unhindered movement of goods, people, and capital was essential. In 1834, at Prussia's initiative, a customs union called the zollverein was formed, joined by most German states. The union abolished tariff barriers and reduced currencies from over thirty to two. The subsequent growth of railways further stimulated mobility and economic integration, harnessing economic interests to the cause of national unification. Economic nationalism strengthened wider nationalist sentiment.

content.title: Friedrich List's Economic Nationalism (1834)
content.text: Economist Friedrich List argued: 'The aim of the zollverein is to bind the Germans economically into a nation. It will strengthen the nation materially by protecting its interests and stimulating internal productivity. It ought to awaken national sentiment through a fusion of individual and provincial interests.' This demonstrates how economic measures became tools for forging national consciousness.

notes.section_title: 2.3 Conservative Reaction After 1815: The Congress of Vienna

content.text: Following Napoleon's defeat in 1815, European governments were driven by conservatism—a philosophy stressing the importance of tradition, established institutions, customs, and gradual development. Conservatives believed that institutions like monarchy, the Church, social hierarchies, property, and family should be preserved. However, most conservatives recognised that modernisation could actually strengthen traditional institutions. A modern, efficient bureaucracy, a dynamic economy, and the abolition of feudalism and serfdom could strengthen autocratic monarchies.

In 1815, representatives of Britain, Russia, Prussia, and Austria—who had collectively defeated Napoleon—met at Vienna to draw up a settlement for Europe. Hosted by Austrian Chancellor Duke Metternich, the Congress aimed to undo most changes that had occurred during the Napoleonic wars. The Treaty of Vienna (1815) restored the Bourbon dynasty to power, had France return annexed territories, and set up a series of buffer states on France's boundaries: the Kingdom of the Netherlands (including Belgium), and the addition of Genoa to Piedmont. Prussia received new territories on its western frontiers, and Austria gained control of northern Italy.

content.title: Main Outcomes of the Congress of Vienna
items: Restoration of the Bourbon dynasty in France
France returned to its pre-revolutionary boundaries
Buffer states created around France to prevent future expansion
German Confederation of 39 states left untouched
Russia granted part of Poland; Prussia given portion of Saxony
Austria given control of northern Italy
Monarchy and conservative order reinforced across Europe

content.title: Conservative Repression
content.text: Conservative regimes were autocratic and intolerant of criticism. They imposed censorship on newspapers, books, plays, and songs—anything reflecting ideas of liberty and freedom associated with the French Revolution. Despite repression, liberal and nationalist ideas continued to inspire opposition movements.

Following 1815, fear of repression drove many liberal-nationalists underground. Secret societies sprang up across European states to train revolutionaries and spread their ideas. Being a revolutionary at this time meant commitment to opposing monarchical forms established after the Vienna Congress and fighting for liberty and freedom. Many revolutionaries saw the creation of nation-states as a necessary part of this struggle.

One such revolutionary was Giuseppe Mazzini, born in Genoa in 1805. As a member of the secret society Carbonari, he attempted revolution in Liguria at age 24 and was sent into exile in 1831. He subsequently founded Young Italy in Marseilles and Young Europe in Berne, whose members were like-minded young men from Poland, France, Italy, and German states. Mazzini believed God had intended nations to be the natural units of mankind. Italy could not remain a patchwork of small states and kingdoms—it must be forged into a single unified republic within a wider alliance of nations. Only then could Italian liberty be achieved.

content.title: Mazzini's Vision and Impact
content.text: Mazzini's relentless opposition to monarchy and his vision of democratic republics frightened conservatives. Metternich called him 'the most dangerous enemy of our social order.' His organisations inspired similar secret societies throughout Europe, spreading revolutionary and nationalist ideology despite official repression.

topics.topic_name: The July Revolution (1830) and Spread of Liberal Uprisings

content.text: As conservative regimes tried to consolidate power, liberalism and nationalism became increasingly associated with revolution in Italian, German, Ottoman, Irish, and Polish territories. These revolutions were led by liberal-nationalists from the educated middle-class elite—professors, schoolteachers, clerks, and commercial middle-class members.

The first major upheaval occurred in France in July 1830. Bourbon kings, restored after 1815, were overthrown by liberal revolutionaries who installed a constitutional monarchy with Louis Philippe as head. Metternich's remark—'When France sneezes, the rest of Europe catches cold'—proved prescient. The July Revolution sparked an uprising in Brussels leading to Belgium's independence from the United Kingdom of the Netherlands. This demonstrated how nationalist and liberal sentiments were interconnected across Europe.

An event that mobilised nationalist feelings across educated Europe was the Greek War of Independence. Greece had been part of the Ottoman Empire since the fifteenth century. The growth of revolutionary nationalism sparked a struggle for independence beginning in 1821. Greek nationalists received support from other Greeks living in exile and from many West Europeans who had romantic sympathy for ancient Greek culture.

Poets and artists lauded Greece as the cradle of European civilisation and mobilised public opinion to support the Greek struggle against the Muslim Ottoman Empire. The English poet Lord Byron organised funds and went to fight in the war, where he died of fever in 1824. The painter Eugène Delacroix's monumental painting 'The Massacre at Chios' (1824) depicted the killing of 20,000 Greeks on the island of Chios, using vivid colours and focus on suffering women and children to appeal to emotions and create sympathy. The Treaty of Constantinople (1832) finally recognised Greece as an independent nation.

content.title: Cause-Effect: Culture and Nationalism
content.text: The Greek case shows how culture—art, poetry, historical narratives—played a crucial role in mobilising nationalist sentiment. Western European romanticisation of ancient Greece provided emotional and intellectual support for Greek independence, demonstrating nationalism's cultural dimensions.

topics.topic_name: 3.1 Romanticism and the Development of National Feeling

content.text: Nationalism developed not only through wars and territorial expansion but also through culture. Art, poetry, stories, and music helped express and shape nationalist feelings. Romanticism—a cultural movement—played a crucial role in developing a particular form of nationalist sentiment. Romantic artists and poets criticised the glorification of reason and science, focusing instead on emotions, intuition, and mystical feelings. Their effort was to create a sense of shared collective heritage and common cultural past as the basis of a nation.

German philosopher Johann Gottfried Herder (1744–1803) claimed that true German culture was to be discovered among the common people—das Volk. Through folk songs, folk poetry, and folk dances, the true spirit of the nation (Volksgeist) was expressed. Collecting and recording these forms of folk culture became essential to nation-building. The Grimm brothers, Jacob and Wilhelm, travelled village to village gathering folktales and published them in 1812. They also compiled a 33-volume dictionary of the German language. Viewing French domination as a threat to German culture, they saw their projects of collecting folktales and developing the German language as part of opposing French dominance and creating German national identity.

content.title: Vernacular Language and Nationalism
content.text: The emphasis on vernacular language and folklore collection served dual purposes: recovering an ancient national spirit and carrying the modern nationalist message to large audiences, often illiterate. This made nationalism accessible and emotionally resonant beyond educated elites.

topics.topic_name: Language as a Tool of National Resistance: The Polish Case

content.text: Poland's experience demonstrates language's role in nationalism. Poland had been partitioned among Russia, Prussia, and Austria at the end of the eighteenth century and no longer existed as an independent territory. Yet national feelings remained alive through music and language. Composer Karol Kurpinski celebrated national struggle through operas and music, incorporating folk dances like the polonaise and mazurka into nationalist symbols.

After Russian occupation, Polish was forced out of schools and Russian imposed everywhere. The 1831 armed rebellion against Russian rule, though crushed, was followed by Polish clergy using language as a weapon of resistance. Polish was used in Church gatherings and all religious instruction. Priests and bishops were imprisoned or sent to Siberia for refusing to preach in Russian. Polish language became a symbol of struggle against Russian dominance, demonstrating how language could embody and sustain national identity even under foreign rule.

The 1830s were years of severe economic hardship. The first half of the nineteenth century saw enormous population increase across Europe. In most countries, job seekers outnumbered available employment. Rural populations migrated to overcrowded urban slums seeking work. Small producers in towns faced stiff competition from cheap, machine-made English goods, where industrialisation was more advanced. This was especially acute in textile production, carried out mainly in homes and small workshops, only partly mechanised. In regions where aristocracy retained power, peasants still struggled under feudal dues and obligations.

Food price increases or poor harvests led to widespread pauperism in towns and countryside. The year 1848 exemplified this: food shortages and widespread unemployment brought Paris to the streets. Barricades were erected, Louis Philippe fled, and the National Assembly proclaimed a Republic, granting suffrage to all adult males above 21 and guaranteeing the right to work. National workshops for employment were established. Earlier, in 1845, Silesian weavers had revolted against contractors who supplied raw materials and reduced payments drastically despite increased workload. The rebellion was brutally suppressed, revealing the desperation of workers in industrialising Europe.

Parallel to poor and working-class revolts, educated middle classes launched their own revolutions in 1848. In France, February events led to the monarchy's abdication and a republic with universal male suffrage. In Germany, Italy, Poland, and the Austro-Hungarian Empire—where independent nation-states did not yet exist—liberal middle-class men and women combined demands for constitutionalism with national unification. They sought nation-states on parliamentary principles with constitutions, press freedom, and associational rights.

In German regions, political associations of middle-class professionals, businessmen, and prosperous artisans gathered in Frankfurt to elect an all-German National Assembly. On 18 May 1848, 831 elected representatives marched in procession to the Frankfurt parliament in the Church of St Paul. They drafted a constitution for a German nation-state to be headed by a monarchy subject to parliament. However, when offered the crown on these terms, King Friedrich Wilhelm IV of Prussia rejected it and joined other monarchs in opposing the assembly. Aristocratic opposition strengthened as the parliament's social basis eroded—the middle-class-dominated assembly resisted workers' and artisans' demands and lost popular support. Troops were called in and the assembly disbanded.

content.title: The Woman Question in 1848
content.text: Women participated actively in 1848 movements, forming political associations, founding newspapers, and attending demonstrations. However, they were denied suffrage rights and excluded from parliament. While liberal ideologies emphasised equality, gender equality was contested. Conservative arguments about 'natural' gender differences justified women's exclusion from political participation, a contradiction that women activists like Louise Otto-Peters challenged, arguing that 'liberty is indivisible.'

notes.section_title: 4. The Making of Germany and Italy

topics.topic_name: 4.1 German Unification: The Role of Prussia and Bismarck

content.text: After 1848, nationalism in Europe moved away from association with democracy and revolution. Nationalist sentiments were now mobilised by conservatives to promote state power and political domination. This is evident in Germany's and Italy's unification processes. German nationalist feeling, widespread among middle-class liberals who attempted in 1848 to unite German regions into a nation-state governed by elected parliament, was suppressed by combined monarchical and military forces supported by large landowners (Junkers).

Prussia subsequently led the unification movement. Its chief minister, Otto von Bismarck, architected this process using the Prussian army and bureaucracy. Three wars over seven years—with Austria (1866), Denmark (1864), and France (1870–71)—ended in Prussian victories and completed unification. In January 1871, the Prussian King William I was proclaimed German Emperor in a ceremony at the Hall of Mirrors in Versailles, symbolising Prussian dominance in the new German state.

content.title: Unification from Above: Conservative Nationalism
content.text: German unification was achieved 'from above'—by state power and military force rather than popular revolution. This demonstrated how nationalism could be appropriated by conservative forces to strengthen state authority. The new German state modernised currency, banking, legal, and judicial systems, with Prussian practices becoming a model for the rest of Germany.

topics.topic_name: 4.2 Italian Unification: Mazzini, Cavour, and Garibaldi

content.text: Like Germany, Italy had a long history of political fragmentation. During the nineteenth century, Italy was divided into seven states: only Sardinia-Piedmont was ruled by an Italian prince; the north was under Austrian Habsburgs; the centre was ruled by the Pope; southern regions were under Spanish Bourbon kings. The Italian language itself had not acquired a common form and retained many regional and local variations. This fragmentation made unification difficult.

During the 1830s, Giuseppe Mazzini sought to develop a coherent programme for a unitary Italian Republic and founded Young Italy to disseminate his goals. The failure of revolutionary uprisings in 1831 and 1848 meant leadership passed to Sardinia-Piedmont under King Victor Emmanuel II. Chief Minister Cavour, neither revolutionary nor democrat but a wealthy, educated elite member who spoke French better than Italian, led the unification effort. Through diplomatic alliance with France engineered by Cavour, Sardinia-Piedmont defeated Austrian forces in 1859. Large numbers of armed volunteers under Giuseppe Garibaldi joined the campaign.

In 1860, Garibaldi and his volunteers marched into South Italy and the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies, winning local peasant support to drive out Spanish rulers. In 1861, Victor Emmanuel II was proclaimed King of unified Italy. However, much of the Italian population, particularly peasants in the south with high illiteracy rates, remained unaware of nationalist ideology. Peasants who supported Garibaldi had never heard of 'Italia' and believed 'La Talia' was Victor Emmanuel's wife—demonstrating that nationalism was still a concept for educated elites rather than the masses.

content.title: The Gap Between Elite and Popular Nationalism
content.text: Italian unification reveals that despite nationalist rhetoric, the masses—especially illiterate peasants—had limited understanding of or identification with the nation-state concept. Unification was largely an elite project, and popular consciousness lagged behind political changes.

The model of the nation-state, some scholars argue, is Great Britain. However, British nationalism developed differently from continental Europe. Before the eighteenth century, there was no 'British nation.' Primary identities were ethnic—English, Welsh, Scot, or Irish. Each ethnic group had distinct political and cultural traditions. As the English nation grew in wealth, importance, and power, it extended influence over other British Isles peoples. The English parliament, which had seized power from monarchy in 1688, became the instrument through which a nation-state centred on England was forged.

The Act of Union (1707) between England and Scotland, forming the 'United Kingdom of Great Britain,' meant England imposed its influence on Scotland. The British parliament became dominated by English members. Growth of British identity meant Scotland's distinctive culture and institutions were systematically suppressed. The Celtic clans of Scottish Highlands suffered terrible repression. Scottish Highlanders were forbidden to speak Gaelic, wear national dress, and many were forcibly driven from their homeland.

Ireland suffered similarly. Deeply divided between Catholics and Protestants, Ireland saw English support for Protestant dominance over the Catholic majority. Catholic revolts against British rule were suppressed. After Wolfe Tone's failed United Irishmen rebellion (1798), Ireland was forcibly incorporated into the United Kingdom (1801). A 'British nation' was forged through propagating dominant English culture. Symbols—the Union Jack flag, national anthem ('God Save Our Noble King'), the English language—were actively promoted. Older nations survived only as subordinate partners in this union.

content.title: Nationalism Through Cultural Dominance
content.text: British nationalism shows how a nation-state could be created through cultural and political dominance of one ethnic group (English) over others, rather than through popular revolutionary movements. This was less violent transformation but still involved suppression of minority identities.

notes.section_title: 5. Visualising the Nation: Symbols and Allegories

content.text: While portraying a ruler through portrait or statue was straightforward, representing an abstract nation was challenging. Artists in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries solved this by personifying nations as female figures. These female forms did not represent actual women but rather the abstract idea of the nation—they were allegories. During the French Revolution, artists used female allegories to portray Liberty, Justice, and the Republic, each with specific attributes: Liberty had a red cap or broken chains; Justice was typically a blindfolded woman with scales.

Nineteenth-century artists invented female allegories for nations. In France, 'Marianne'—a popular Christian name underscoring the people's nation—was chosen. Her characteristics derived from Liberty and the Republic: the red cap, tricolour, and cockade. Statues of Marianne were erected in public squares to remind people of national unity. Marianne's image appeared on coins and stamps, making the allegorical figure a constant presence in daily life, reinforcing national consciousness.

Similarly, 'Germania' became the allegory of the German nation. In visual representations, Germania wore a crown of oak leaves—the German oak symbolising heroism. Philip Veit prepared a monumental painting of Germania in 1848 on a cotton banner hung in the Church of St Paul where the Frankfurt parliament convened. The painting's symbolism was rich: she held a sword (readiness to fight), wore a crown of oak leaves (heroism), and rays of a rising sun suggested the beginning of a new era. An earlier Veit rendering (1836) placed a Kaiser's crown where the later version showed a broken chain—a significant change reflecting the shift from imperial aspirations to liberty symbolism between 1836 and 1848.

content.title: Symbolic Meanings in National Allegories
items: Broken chains: Being freed from oppression
Breastplate with eagle: Symbol of national strength
Crown of oak leaves: Heroism and national pride
Sword: Readiness to fight for the nation
Olive branch around sword: Willingness to make peace
Black, red, and gold tricolour: Flag of liberal-nationalists in 1848 German states
Rays of rising sun: Beginning of a new era
Red cap: Revolutionary ideals and liberty

content.title: The Power of Visual Symbols
content.text: National allegories were powerful tools for creating and sustaining national consciousness. By making abstract concepts concrete and emotionally resonant through visual art distributed on coins, stamps, and public monuments, governments and artists shaped how people imagined and identified with the nation.

notes.section_title: 6. Nationalism and Imperialism: Late Nineteenth-Century Developments

content.text: By the last quarter of the nineteenth century, nationalism had transformed. It no longer retained the idealistic liberal-democratic sentiment of the early nineteenth century but became a narrow creed with limited, often exclusive ends. Nationalist groups became increasingly intolerant of each other and ready for war. Major European powers manipulated nationalist aspirations of subject peoples to further their own imperialist aims, linking nationalism with imperial domination rather than popular liberation.

topics.topic_name: The Balkans: A Region of Conflict

content.text: The Balkans became the most serious source of nationalist tension after 1871. This region of great geographical and ethnic variation comprises modern-day Romania, Bulgaria, Albania, Greece, Macedonia, Croatia, Bosnia-Herzegovina, Slovenia, Serbia, and Montenegro. Its inhabitants, broadly known as Slavs, were very diverse. Much of the Balkans was under Ottoman Empire control. The spread of romantic nationalism in the Balkans, combined with the Ottoman Empire's disintegration, made the region volatile. All through the nineteenth century, the Ottoman Empire sought to strengthen itself through modernisation and reforms but with little success. One by one, its European subject nationalities broke away and declared independence.

Balkan rebels justified claims for independence or political rights based on nationality and used history to argue they had once been independent but were subsequently subjugated by foreign powers. They sought to 'win back' their long-lost independence. However, as different Slavic nationalities struggled to define their identity and independence, the Balkans became an area of intense conflict. Balkan states were fiercely jealous of each other, each hoping to gain territory at others' expense. This was further complicated by big-power rivalry: Russia, Germany, England, and Austro-Hungary each sought to counter others' influence and extend their own control. This competition over trade, colonies, naval might, and Balkan influence led to series of wars in the region and ultimately contributed to the outbreak of the First World War in 1914.

content.title: Nationalism and Imperialism Interconnected
content.text: The Balkans case demonstrates how nationalism became entangled with imperialism. European powers manipulated Balkan nationalist movements to serve their imperial interests, transforming nationalism from an ideology of popular liberation into a tool of great-power domination. This ultimately destabilised Europe, contributing to the First World War.

notes.section_title: Conclusion: Global Implications of European Nationalism

topics.topic_name: The Spread of Nationalism Beyond Europe

content.text: Nationalism aligned with imperialism led Europe into disaster in 1914. However, many countries colonised by European powers in the nineteenth century began opposing imperial domination in the twentieth century. Anti-imperial movements that developed everywhere were nationalist in character—peoples struggled to form independent nation-states and were inspired by collective national unity forged in confrontation with imperialism. While European nationalist ideas were replicated globally, people everywhere developed their own specific varieties of nationalism reflecting local histories and circumstances. Nonetheless, the idea that societies should be organised as nation-states—an eighteenth and nineteenth-century European development—came to be accepted as natural and universal worldwide.

glossary.definition: A form of monarchical government that was centralised, militarised, and repressive, with no restraints on the power exercised by the ruler.
A literary, artistic, or symbolic representation of abstract ideas through persons or things; a work with both literal and symbolic meanings.
A political philosophy that stressed the importance of tradition, established institutions, customs, and preferred gradual development to quick change.
Relating to a common racial, tribal, cultural origin or background that a community identifies with or claims as its own.
Awareness of women's rights and interests based on the belief of social, economic, and political equality of the genders.
A system of ideas reflecting a particular social and political vision or worldview.
A political ideology emphasising individual freedom, equality before the law, and government by consent; opposition to autocracy and clerical privileges.
A community of people bound together by shared history, culture, language, or collective will; distinguished from ethnic groups by the addition of collective consciousness and will.
A political unit where a centralised power exercises sovereign control over a defined territory and where the majority of citizens share a sense of collective identity.
A political ideology and movement asserting the importance of national identity and the right of peoples to form independent nation-states.
A direct vote by which all the people of a region are asked to accept or reject a proposal.
A cultural movement emphasising emotion, intuition, and mystical feelings over reason and science; playing an important role in developing nationalist sentiment.
The right to vote in political elections.
A vision of a society that is so ideal that it is unlikely to actually exist; an idealistic but impractical vision.
The spirit of the people; according to Romantic theorists like Herder, the authentic soul of a nation expressed in folk culture.

summary_points: Nationalism emerged in nineteenth-century Europe as a powerful force transforming political landscapes, moving from early liberal-democratic ideals toward conservative state power and eventually imperial domination.

The French Revolution (1789) provided the first clear expression of nationalism by transferring sovereignty from monarchy to citizens and implementing measures to create collective national identity through symbols, language, laws, and administration.

The spread of French revolutionary and Napoleonic ideas across Europe sparked both enthusiasm for liberation and resistance to foreign domination, creating complex reactions to nationalist ideology among diverse European populations.

Liberal nationalism combined the ideology of liberalism (individual freedom, equality before law, representative government) with the pursuit of national unification and independence, particularly among educated middle classes.

Romantic culture—including art, poetry, folk traditions, and language preservation—played a crucial role in creating emotional attachments to nations and making nationalism accessible to non-elites through vernacular expressions of national identity.

The Congress of Vienna (1815) represented conservative reaction against revolutionary nationalism, attempting to restore traditional monarchies and prevent further upheavals, yet failed to suppress growing nationalist and liberal sentiments.

Secret societies like Young Italy and Young Europe, led by figures like Giuseppe Mazzini, kept revolutionary nationalist ideals alive despite conservative repression and prepared the ground for revolutions of 1830 and 1848.

The Age of Revolutions (1830–1848) saw liberal-nationalist uprisings across Europe, with the middle classes demanding constitutions, national unification, and representative government, though most were ultimately suppressed by conservative forces.

After 1848, nationalism was appropriated by conservative forces and used to strengthen state power; Germany and Italy were unified 'from above' through state power and military force rather than popular revolution.

German unification under Prussian leadership and Italian unification demonstrated how nationalist sentiment could be mobilised for conservative state-building, with limited participation from the masses, particularly illiterate peasants.

British nationalism developed uniquely through cultural and political dominance of England over Scotland, Wales, and Ireland, creating a hierarchical 'United Kingdom' rather than a popular nation-state.

National allegories—particularly female figures like Marianne and Germania—were powerful tools for visualising and personalising abstract nationalist concepts, creating emotional connections between citizens and the nation-state.

By the late nineteenth century, nationalism had transformed from liberatory ideology into a narrow, competitive nationalism linked to imperial domination, with major powers manipulating nationalist movements to further their own interests.

The Balkans became a flashpoint of nationalist and imperial rivalries, with competing Slavic nationalisms and great-power intervention destabilising the region and contributing to the outbreak of World War I.

The idea of the nation-state—an eighteenth and nineteenth-century European development—became globally accepted as the 'natural' form of political organisation, though anti-colonial nationalisms would eventually challenge European imperial power using this same ideology.
